result:
The soft hum of rain against the window always pulls me into that peculiar state where time loosens its grip. I’ve been thinking lately about how memory works like water—slipping through fingers, yet somehow carving entire canyons into who we are.

Yesterday I found an old notebook from when I was twenty-two, filled with embarrassingly earnest poetry about love and cosmic loneliness. The handwriting was frantic, almost feral. Reading it now feels like meeting a much younger version of myself who hadn't yet learned how to be afraid of looking ridiculous. There's something beautiful about that—how we accumulate these previous selves like nested dolls, each one thinking they finally figured it out, only to be proven sweetly wrong by time.

I’ve been experimenting with making sourdough lately, and there's something meditative about watching the starter bubble and breathe on my counter. It reminds me that growth doesn't always look dramatic. Sometimes it's just this quiet, smelly persistence in a jar.

The world feels both too large and too small these days. We carry universes in our pockets while forgetting to look at the actual sky. I've started collecting moments instead of things— the particular way my neighbor laughs at her dog's antics, the smell of coffee shops at 7:13am, how certain songs hit different when you're driving through rain at night.

Maybe that's what we're all doing here—trying to make meaning from the beautiful mess of being temporarily alive. And isn't it wild that we get to do it together?

(Word count: 248)

Yesterday I found an old notebook from when I was twenty-two, filled with embarrassingly earnest poetry about love and cosmic loneliness. The handwriting was frantic, almost feral. Reading it now feels like meeting a much younger version of myself who hadn't yet learned how to be afraid of looking ridiculous. There's something beautiful about that—how we accumulate these previous selves like nested dolls, each one thinking they finally figured it out, only to be proven sweetly wrong by time.

I’ve been experimenting with making sourdough lately, and there's something meditative about watching the starter bubble and breathe on my counter. It reminds me that growth doesn't always look dramatic. Sometimes it's just this quiet, smelly persistence in a jar.

The world feels both too large and too small these days. We carry universes in our pockets while forgetting to look at the actual sky. I've started collecting moments instead of things— the particular way my neighbor laughs at her dog's antics, the smell of coffee shops at 7:13am, how certain songs hit different when you're driving through rain at night.

Maybe that's what we're all doing here—trying to make meaning from the beautiful mess of being temporarily alive. And isn't it wild that we get to do it together?
